stories_covered: Anthropic raises $15B from Microsoft and Nvidia at $350B valuation
Google launches Gemini 3 Pro with reported advantages on design tasks
Vertical integration: foundation models building own data centers
Nvidia customer concentration risk (4-5 customers represent 70-80% of revenue)
harry_takes: There is no stability in the model space—weekly Twitter sentiment swings between Gemini, ChatGPT, and Claude show the market hasn't settled
Nvidia's extreme customer concentration (70-80% revenue from 4-5 hyperscalers) makes them vulnerable to any single customer building their own chips
Google, Meta, and Amazon will eventually build their own chips; when they do, Nvidia loses those customers and massive margin dollars
The structural risk of chip substitution is more existential than any near-term competition between models
harry_boldest_quote: There's just no stability in seed investing.
predictions: Lemkin: Google will offer TPUs to other cloud providers to defend against Nvidia concentration
Rory: At least one of Google/Meta/Amazon will build a chip good enough to 50% reduce their Nvidia spend within 5 years
Harry: Nvidia's margin profile becomes permanently compressed if even one large customer achieves 70% cost parity on own silicon
Rory: Credit market signals (Oracle CDS spike) suggest growing anxiety about overlevered CapEx plays
disagreements: Lemkin acknowledged the TPU risk but downplayed its probability; Rory and Harry treated it as imminent and existential. Lemkin emphasized model capability differentiation; Harry and Rory focused on infrastructure concentration as the primary risk.
harry_role: Harry repeatedly surfaced the Nvidia concentration risk and forced the panel to quantify the customer defection threat. He pivoted away from model performance debates and anchored on the structural/economic risk of vertical integration.
notable: The group discovered that if Nvidia is spending 40% of Google's $90B capex budget ($36B), Google is essentially handing Nvidia $20B+ in annual profit—a threshold at which chip building becomes economically rational even for a $1-5B development spend.
